The power for the cross is generated from the ground up, originating from a strong push off the ball of the rear foot.[[84]](#cite_note-ultimate-86)[[86]](#cite_note-join-88) For additional power, the torso and hips are rotated counter-clockwise as the cross is thrown.[[84]](#cite_note-ultimate-86)[[86]](#cite_note-join-88) A measure of an ideally extended cross is that the shoulder of the striking arm, the knee of the front leg and the ball of the front foot are on the same vertical plane.[[91]](#cite_note-93) Weight is also transferred from the rear foot to the lead foot, resulting in the rear heel turning outwards as it acts as a fulcrum for the transfer of weight.[[86]](#cite_note-join-88) Like the jab, a half-step forward may be added. After the straight is thrown, the hand is retracted quickly and the guard position resumed.[[84]](#cite_note-ultimate-86)[[86]](#cite_note-join-88) The straight sets up the lead hook well. The Cross can also follow a jab, creating the classic "one-two combo."[[83]](#cite_note-combo-85) When the same punch is used to counter a jab, aiming for the opponent's head it is called a "cross" or "[cross-counter](/wiki/Cross-counter "Cross-counter")". A cross-counter is a [counterpunch](/wiki/Counterpunch_(boxing) "Counterpunch (boxing)") begun immediately after an opponent throws a jab, exploiting the opening in the opponent's position.
* **Hook** – A semi-circular punch thrown with the lead hand to the side of the opponent's head. From the guard position, the elbow is drawn back with a horizontal fist (palm facing down) though in modern times a wide percentage of fighters throw the hook with a vertical fist (palm facing themselves). The rear hand is tucked firmly against the jaw to protect the chin. The torso and hips are rotated clockwise, propelling the fist through a tight, clockwise arc across the front of the body and connecting with the target.
  + At the same time, the lead foot pivots clockwise, turning the left heel outwards. Upon contact, the hook's circular path ends abruptly and the lead hand is pulled quickly back into the guard position. A hook may also target the lower body and this technique is sometimes called the "rip" to distinguish it from the conventional hook to the head. The hook may also be thrown with the rear hand. Notable left hookers include Joe Frazier, Roy Jones Jr. and Mike Tyson.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Boxing080905_photoshop.jpg/250px-Boxing080905_photoshop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxing080905_photoshop.jpg)

[Ricardo Dominguez](/wiki/Ricardo_Dominguez "Ricardo Dominguez") (left) throws an [uppercut](/wiki/Uppercut "Uppercut") on Rafael Ortiz (right)[[92]](#cite_note-94)

* **Uppercut** – A vertical, rising punch thrown with the rear hand. From the guard position, the torso shifts slightly to the right, the rear hand drops below the level of the opponent's chest and the knees are bent slightly. From this position, the rear hand is thrust upwards in a rising arc towards the opponent's chin or torso.
  + At the same time, the knees push upwards quickly and the torso and hips rotate anti-clockwise and the rear heel turns outward, mimicking the body movement of the cross. The strategic utility of the uppercut depends on its ability to "lift" an opponent's body, setting it off-balance for successive attacks. The right uppercut followed by a left hook is a deadly combination employing the uppercut to lift an opponent's chin into a vulnerable position, then the hook to knock the opponent out. Both the hook and uppercut may be thrown with both hands, resulting in differing footwork and positioning from that described above if thrown by the other hand. Generally the analogous opposite is true of the footwork and torso movement.
* **[Overhand](/wiki/Drop_(boxing) "Drop (boxing)")** — The overhand punch, also known as a drop or overcut, is a powerful, semi-circular strike thrown in a vertical, arcing motion designed to go over an opponent's guard or strike, like a jab, to hit their head.[[82]](#cite_note-dynamic_2-84)[[93]](#cite_note-overhand-95)
  + Executed by driving off the back leg and dropping the body weight into the punch, its mechanics involve a coordinated step and weight transfer similar to throwing a baseball to generate significant power.[[93]](#cite_note-overhand-95) Depending on the fighter's stance, the footwork varies to either maintain a wide base for a quick retreat or to step in for more power and balance, though the punch often leaves the thrower exposed, requiring a defensive roll to avoid counters.[[93]](#cite_note-overhand-95)

These different punch types can be thrown in rapid succession to form combinations or "combos". The most common is the jab and cross combination, nicknamed the "[one-two combo](/wiki/One-two_combo "One-two combo")". This is usually an effective combination, because the jab blocks the opponent's view of the cross, making it easier to land cleanly and forcefully.

A large, swinging circular punch starting from a cocked-back position with the arm at a longer extension than the hook and all of the fighter's weight behind it is sometimes referred to as a "roundhouse", "haymaker", or sucker-punch. A haymaker is a wide-angle punch similar to a hook, but instead of getting power from body rotation, it gets its power from its large loop. It is considered an unsophisticated punch, and leaves one open to a counter.[[94]](#cite_note-bjj-96)[[95]](#cite_note-mma-97) Relying on body weight and centripetal force within a wide arc, the roundhouse can be a powerful blow, but it is often a wild and uncontrolled punch that leaves the fighter delivering it off balance and with an open guard.

Wide, looping punches have the further disadvantage of taking more time to deliver, giving the opponent ample warning to react and counter. For this reason, the haymaker or roundhouse is not a conventional punch, and is regarded by trainers as a mark of poor technique or desperation. Sometimes it has been used, because of its immense potential power, to finish off an already staggering opponent who seems unable or unlikely to take advantage of the poor position it leaves the puncher in.

Another unconventional punch is the rarely used [bolo punch](/wiki/Bolo_punch "Bolo punch"), in which the opponent swings an arm out several times in a wide arc, usually as a distraction, before delivering with either that or the other arm.

An illegal punch to the back of the head or neck is known as a [rabbit punch](/wiki/Rabbit_punch "Rabbit punch").

### Defense

Defense in boxing refers to actions taken by a boxer to avoid being hit, redirect an opponents attack or reduce the impact of punches to vital areas such as the head. Defensive techniques generally fall into 4 categories of evading, blocking, covering and clinching.

### Evading

Evading refers to actions a boxer takes to try to avoid strikes entirely by making their opponents miss.

* [![Slipping](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Slip1.jpg/120px-Slip1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slip1.jpg "Slipping")

  *[Slipping](/wiki/Slipping "Slipping")*
* [![Bobbing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Slip2.jpg/120px-Slip2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slip2.jpg "Bobbing")

  *[Bobbing](/wiki/Bobbing_(boxing) "Bobbing (boxing)")*
* [![Footwork](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Pas_de_retrait.jpg/120px-Pas_de_retrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pas_de_retrait.jpg "Footwork")

  *[Footwork](/wiki/Footwork_(martial_arts) "Footwork (martial arts)")*
* [![Pulling away](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Retrait2.jpg/120px-Retrait2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Retrait2.jpg "Pulling away")

  *Pulling away*
* [![Shoulder roll](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Bloc_%C3%A9paule1.jpg/120px-Bloc_%C3%A9paule1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bloc_%C3%A9paule1.jpg "Shoulder roll")

  *[Shoulder roll](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Shoulder_roll "Crab Defense (boxing style)")*

* **Slipping** — involves moving the head slightly offline of an incoming punch, often by leaning and twisting the upper body.
* **[Bob-and-weave](/wiki/Bob_and_weave "Bob and weave")** — bobbing moves the head laterally and beneath an incoming punch. As the opponent's punch arrives, the boxer bends the legs quickly and simultaneously shifts the body either slightly right or left. Once the punch has been evaded, the boxer "weaves" back to an upright position, emerging on either the outside or inside of the opponent's still-extended arm. To move outside the opponent's extended arm is called "bobbing to the outside". To move inside the opponent's extended arm is called "bobbing to the inside".
* **Footwork** — involves moving the feet to create angles, create distance, or get out of the way of punches, including linear and circular movements.
* **Pulling** — Moving the body backward to create distance and avoid punches.
* **Leaning back** — moving the upper body backward to evade punches, often combined with shifting weight onto the back leg.
* **Sway / fade** — To anticipate a punch and move the upper body or head back so that it misses or has its force appreciably lessened. Also called "rolling with the punch" or "riding the punch".
* **Shoulder roll** – To execute the shoulder roll a fighter rotates and ducks (to the right for orthodox fighters and to the left for southpaws) when their opponents punch is coming towards them and then rotates back towards their opponent while their opponent is bringing their hand back. The fighter will throw a punch with their back hand as they are rotating towards their undefended opponent.

### Blocking

Blocking refers to actions a boxer takes to absorb, redirect, intercept or slow the momentum of an opponents strikes preventing blows from impacting vital areas such as the head and midsection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Side_Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag113%29.jpg/250px-Side_Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag113%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Side_Parry_(Boxing;_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag113).jpg)

Boxer (left) parrying opponent's jab from closed stance.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Dodge_with_Deviation_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag114%29.jpg/250px-Dodge_with_Deviation_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag114%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dodge_with_Deviation_(Boxing;_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag114).jpg)

Boxer (right) parrying opponent's jab from open stance.

* **Parry** — parrying uses the boxer's hands as defensive tools to deflect incoming attacks.[[96]](#cite_note-block-98) As the opponent's punch arrives, the boxer delivers a sharp, lateral, open-handed blow to the opponent's wrist or forearm, redirecting the punch.[[96]](#cite_note-block-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Low_Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag115%29.jpg/250px-Low_Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag115%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Low_Parry_(Boxing;_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag115).jpg)

Boxer (left) using a low parry.

* **Low parry** — is a defensive technique used to deflect punches aimed at the body, particularly low punches. It involves moving the arm in a half-circle motion, typically starting from the outside and moving inwards, to clear the punch to the side. This technique is effective because it avoids absorbing the impact from the punch directly, which can be more forceful and put you off balance, instead, it guides the punch away from the intended target.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Boxing_at_the_2015_European_Games_4.jpg/250px-Boxing_at_the_2015_European_Games_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxing_at_the_2015_European_Games_4.jpg)

Boxer (right) using a punch catch

* **Punch catch** — is a defensive technique where a fighter uses their open palm to intercept an incoming punch, aiming to slow the momentum of the strike and stopping it from hitting its intended target.[[96]](#cite_note-block-98) Catching is often used for straight punches like the jab.[[96]](#cite_note-block-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d9/EVD-boxeo-090.jpg)](/wiki/File:EVD-boxeo-090.jpg)

Boxer (right) using uppercut catch from open stance

* **Uppercut catch** — is a defensive technique where a fighter uses their open palm to intercept an incoming uppercut, aiming to slow the momentum of the strike and stopping it from hitting its intended target. This is generally used against uppercuts to the head. In general when boxers are in a closed stance the boxer uses their rear hand to catch a lead uppercut and their lead hand to catch a rear uppercut. In an open stance the boxer generally uses their lead hand to catch a lead uppercut and their rear hand to catch a rear uppercut.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/John_Wright_%28GB%29_vs_Mick_McKeon_%28Eire%29%2C_1948_London_Olympic_Boxing_Semi-final-2.jpg/250px-John_Wright_%28GB%29_vs_Mick_McKeon_%28Eire%29%2C_1948_London_Olympic_Boxing_Semi-final-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Wright_(GB)_vs_Mick_McKeon_(Eire),_1948_London_Olympic_Boxing_Semi-final-2.jpg)

Boxer (left) using cross block

* **[Cross block](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Cross_block "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** — is often done with the rear arm (right for an orthodox fighter and left for a [southpaw](/wiki/Southpaw_stance "Southpaw stance")) but can also be done with the lead arm (left for an orthodox fighter and right for a southpaw). In a cross block position with the rear hand, the glove is over the lead shoulder with the palm facing towards the opponent. Using the lead hand the glove is over the rear shoulder with the palm facing towards the opponent. With the cross block the glove is usually used to block straight punches, but the forearm can also be used. The forearm and elbow can be used to block uppercuts, and the glove and elbow can also be used to block hooks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag77%29.jpg/250px-Parry_%28Boxing%3B_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag77%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parry_(Boxing;_a_guide_to_the_manly_art_of_self_defense_pag77).jpg)

Boxer (left) using a wedge block

* **[Wedge block](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Wedge_block "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** — also known as the horizontal forearm block or leverage block. This block is used primarily with the lead arm to defend against straight punches by moving the arm upwards towards the incoming punch.[[97]](#cite_note-wedge_block-99)[[98]](#cite_note-lead_hand-100)[[99]](#cite_note-12_boxing-101) It can be used against hooks by moving the arm up and outwards towards the incoming hook, or outwards to jam [uppercuts](/wiki/Uppercut "Uppercut") in boxing.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

* [![Blocking (straight punch to the body with the forearms)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Blocage1.jpg/120px-Blocage1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blocage1.jpg "Blocking (straight punch to the body with the forearms)")

  *[Blocking](/wiki/Blocking_(martial_arts) "Blocking (martial arts)") (straight punch to the body with the forearms)*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a5/EVD-boxeo-082.jpg)](/wiki/File:EVD-boxeo-082.jpg)

Fighter (right) using forearm block against uppercut to the body

* **Forearm body blocks** — Boxers, especially classic guard fighters, will often turn their body towards straight strikes and uppercuts to the midsection using their forearms to block.
* **Elbow body blocks** — Boxers often use their elbows to block hooks to the liver and kidneys by moving their elbows or leaning their bodies so the elbow connects with their opponent's fists.[[101]](#cite_note-boxing_101-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/WAKO-PRO_Weltmeisterschaft_309.jpg/250px-WAKO-PRO_Weltmeisterschaft_309.jpg)](/wiki/File:WAKO-PRO_Weltmeisterschaft_309.jpg)

Fighter (left) using reverse elbow block

* **[Reverse elbow block](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Elbow_block "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** — Crab Style fighters are unique as the low lead allows them to use the reverse elbows to block their heads.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104)[[103]](#cite_note-explained-105) The reverse elbow block can be used from a shoulder roll position.[[104]](#cite_note-his_elbow-106) The reverse elbow block also functions as an intermediating position between a wedge block and a shoulder roll, allowing a boxer to move from a reverse elbow block to a wedge block or shoulder roll.
* **Shoulder block** — a defensive technique where a fighter uses their shoulder to deflect or block punches, particularly the opponent's lead hand punch like a right cross or a southpaw jab. The fighter positions their lead shoulder high, tucking their chin behind it. The shoulder is rolled forward to meet the incoming punch, deflecting it away from the head and body.

> "If, however, his right lead is thrown at you when you are out of normal position-when, for example, you have permitted your left hand to drop down in an overzealous feint to the body-you must block with your left shoulder. You give your left shoulder a frantic, whirling hunch to protect
> your already snuggled chin. Thus, the blow thuds into your shoulder instead of into your face (Figure 53). You'll be tempted to use your right hand to help your left shoulder in that block. You'll be tempted to make a "shell defense" with shoulder and hand. But don't do it. You've got to keep that right hand in its normal position, ready to (1) guard against the possibility of a following left hook, and (2) smash a straight right counter to your opponent's solar plexus or chin." - [Jack Dempsey](/wiki/Jack_Dempsey "Jack Dempsey")'s Championship Boxing Explosive Punching and Aggressive Defense.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

### Covering

Covering refers to action a boxer takes to reduce the impact of strikes to vital areas such as the head and midsection. Unlike blocking, covering puts the gloves on the boxer's head or body directly. Some damage is still done to the boxer while covering, but the goal is to reduce the damage by using the gloves or arms as shock absorbers lessening the severity of blows.

* [![Covering (with the gloves)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Protection_passive1.jpg/120px-Protection_passive1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protection_passive1.jpg "Covering (with the gloves)")

  *[Covering](/wiki/Covering_(martial_arts) "Covering (martial arts)") (with the gloves)*

* **Covering** – covering up is the last opportunity to avoid an incoming strike to an unprotected face or body. Generally speaking, the hands are held high to protect the head and chin and the forearms are tucked against the torso to impede body shots. When protecting the body, the boxer rotates the hips and lets incoming punches "roll" off the guard. To protect the head, the boxer presses both fists against the front of the face with the forearms parallel and facing outwards. This type of guard is weak against attacks from below.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/EVD-boxeo-101.jpg/250px-EVD-boxeo-101.jpg)](/wiki/File:EVD-boxeo-101.jpg)

Boxer (left) using hook cover

* **Hook cover** – a hook cover is a defense against a hook where a boxer raises their hand up, bending the elbow as if answering a phone creating a position where the glove covers the head against the hook.[[106]](#cite_note-aggressive56-108) The chin is also tucked while covering.[[106]](#cite_note-aggressive56-108) The boxer may also slightly lean the upper body away from the incoming hook, coordinating this lean with a small step or shift in their weight to maintain balance and create space for a counter.[[106]](#cite_note-aggressive56-108)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_%2815097099343%29.jpg/250px-DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_%2815097099343%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_(15097099343).jpg)

Boxer (right) using a Helmet cover

* **Helmet cover** – also known as a Hammer cover, is a variation of the Hook cover. It is a defensive technique where a fighter raises their forearm and hand to protect their head, it resembles a person using a hammer.[[107]](#cite_note-hammer-109) This technique is often used when facing opponents who throw high-impact punches to defend against hooks and overhands.[[107]](#cite_note-hammer-109)

### Clinching

Clinching refers to grappling techniques a boxer uses to tie up an opponent's arms to prevent them from striking, or lessen the impact of strikes. Clinching techniques can also be used to move an opponent to a position where they are unable to effectively strike from. Clinching also includes framing, pinning, posting and trapping an opponent's hand or arm to prevent them from punching.[[108]](#cite_note-hand-110)[[109]](#cite_note-apollo-111)[[110]](#cite_note-clinch-112)

* [![Clinching](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Neutraliser1.jpg/120px-Neutraliser1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neutraliser1.jpg "Clinching")

  *[Clinching](/wiki/Clinch_fighting "Clinch fighting")*

* **[Clinch](/wiki/Stand-up_grappling_position "Stand-up grappling position")** – clinching is a rough form of [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling") and occurs when the distance between both fighters has closed and straight punches cannot be employed. In this situation, the boxer attempts to [hold](/wiki/Grappling_hold "Grappling hold") or ["tie up"](/wiki/Grappling_hold#Clinch_hold "Grappling hold") the opponent's hands so he is unable to throw hooks or uppercuts. To perform a clinch, the boxer loops both hands around the outside of the opponent's shoulders, scooping back under the forearms to grasp the opponent's arms tightly against his own body. In this position, the opponent's arms are pinned and cannot be used to attack. Clinching is a temporary match state and is quickly dissipated by the referee.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._%2829027970771%29.jpg/250px-Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._%2829027970771%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._(29027970771).jpg)

Boxer (left) using Arm-in hug against boxer (right) using High Guard

* **Arm-in hug** usually occurs when the opponent is in a high guard while changing levels to enter the clinch. The arms are wrapped around the opponent, covering the whole body. This action traps their arms on the inside, preventing them from punching. The arm-in hug is a rather weak position that should not be relied on too much as an opponent can easily break out of it by pushing, or putting a frame with the forearm or elbow.[[110]](#cite_note-clinch-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Johnson-Jeffries-1910-NMAAHC-2010_36_1_2_001.jpg/250px-Johnson-Jeffries-1910-NMAAHC-2010_36_1_2_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johnson-Jeffries-1910-NMAAHC-2010_36_1_2_001.jpg)

James J. Jeffries (right) using underhooks against Jack Johnson

* **[Underhook](/wiki/Underhook "Underhook")** is a position that a boxer may use in a clinch. The boxer's arm is placed under their opponent's arm or armpit. Their hand can be placed on their upper arm, shoulder or back. It is often used in combination with other arm positions such as an [overhook](/wiki/Overhook "Overhook") which is called an [over-under position](/wiki/Over-under_position "Over-under position"). When a boxer secures one underhook it is called a single underhook and when using both underhooks it is called double underhooks. An underhook can be used to push the opponent's arm down or lift the opponent up and destabilize them, breaking their balance and getting them off their base.[[110]](#cite_note-clinch-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Clinch%2C_Pete_McCoy_and_Johnny_Reagan%2C_from_the_Boxing_Positions_and_Boxers_series_%28N266%29_issued_by_P._Lorillard_Company_to_promote_Red_Cross_Long_Cut_Tobacco_MET_DPB879440.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clinch,_Pete_McCoy_and_Johnny_Reagan,_from_the_Boxing_Positions_and_Boxers_series_(N266)_issued_by_P._Lorillard_Company_to_promote_Red_Cross_Long_Cut_Tobacco_MET_DPB879440.jpg)

Boxer (left) using collar tie against boxer (right) using cross collar tie

* **[Collar tie](/wiki/Collar_tie "Collar tie")** also known as the head pull, is a clinch technique.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) From a closed stance the boxer uses the lead hand to grab the opponent's rear side collar or the back of their neck and their forearm presses against the opponent's collarbone or the back of their neck to control their posture and head movement.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113)[[112]](#cite_note-mitt-114) If the boxer uses their rear hand in a closed stance they would grab their opponents lead side. The goal is to control the opponent's head by bending it down.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113)[[112]](#cite_note-mitt-114) This allows the boxer to set up attacks like uppercuts and hooks, or to create angles.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) A properly executed collar tie involves pressing the elbow to the chest and using the forearm to create a strong frame, preventing the opponent from escaping or generating power for their own attacks.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) When one collar tie is used it is called a single collar tie and when two collar ties are used it is called a double collar tie.
* **Cross collar tie** also known as a forearm smash, is a clinch technique.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) From a closed stance the boxer uses the lead hand to go across their body to grab the opponent's lead side collar, or the back of their neck and their forearm presses against the opponent's collarbone or the back of their neck to control their posture and head movement.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) If the boxer uses their rear hand in a closed stance they would cross their body and grab their opponents rear side. The goal is to control the opponent's head by bending it down and to the side. This allows the boxer to set up attacks like uppercuts and hooks, or to create angles. A properly executed cross collar tie involves using the forearm to create a strong frame, preventing the opponent from escaping or generating power for their own attacks.[[111]](#cite_note-tricks-113) The boxer can also grab the opponent's shoulder and pull it down and to the side in the same way as they would against their opponent's head.[[113]](#cite_note-pad-115) The cross collar tie is often used with an elbow tie on the same side to keep an opponent from punching and allowing the boxer to circle outside of their opponent.[[113]](#cite_note-pad-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/In_Chancery%2C_George_Godfrey_and_Joe_Lannon%2C_from_the_Boxing_Positions_and_Boxers_series_%28N266%29_issued_by_P._Lorillard_Company_to_promote_Red_Cross_Long_Cut_Tobacco_MET_DPB879436.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:In_Chancery,_George_Godfrey_and_Joe_Lannon,_from_the_Boxing_Positions_and_Boxers_series_(N266)_issued_by_P._Lorillard_Company_to_promote_Red_Cross_Long_Cut_Tobacco_MET_DPB879436.jpg)

Boxer (right) using front headlock

* **Front headlock** or chancery, is when a fighter secures a clinch, then uses their shoulder and arm to lock the opponent's head under their armpit. An opponent will often go for a headlock to get out of a defensive body lock that has been applied. To defend against this headlock, one should walk their hips under for a straighter posture and use their legs to lift up. This action will either force the opponent to release the grip or lift them off their feet.[[110]](#cite_note-clinch-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_%2815716397925%29.jpg/250px-DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_%2815716397925%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:DF_Boxing_Championships_2014_(15716397925).jpg)

Boxer (right) using framing against boxer (left) using High Guard

* **Framing** is a defensive technique where a boxer uses their hand, forearm, or body to control an opponent's position, create distance, or disrupt their balance. By establishing a physical barrier, framing can prevent punches, set up counters, manipulate an opponent's guard, or create openings for a boxer's own attacks. Boxing utilizes different frames, including entrance frames for closing distance and exit frames for creating space after an attack.[[108]](#cite_note-hand-110)[[109]](#cite_note-apollo-111)

### Guards

There are 4 main **defensive positions (guards or styles)** used in boxing:

All fighters have their own variations to these styles. Some fighters may have their guard higher for more head protection while others have their guard lower to provide better protection against body punches. Many fighters don't strictly use a single position, but rather adapt to the situation when choosing a certain position to protect them.[[114]](#cite_note-Argo-116)

**[Peek-a-Boo](/wiki/Peek-a-Boo_(boxing_style) "Peek-a-Boo (boxing style)")** — a defensive style often used by a fighter where the hands are placed in front of the boxer's face,[[48]](#cite_note-auto1-50) like in the babies' game of the same name. It offers extra protection to the face and makes it easier to jab the opponent's face. Peek-a-Boo boxing was developed by legendary trainer [Cus D'Amato](/wiki/Cus_D%27Amato "Cus D'Amato"). Peek-a-Boo boxing utilizes relaxed hands with the forearms in front of the face and the fist at nose-eye level. Other unique features includes side to side head movements, bobbing, weaving and blind siding your opponent. The number system e.g. 3-2-3-Body-head-body or 3-3-2 Body-Body-head is drilled with a stationary dummy called the "Willie bag", named by Cus after boxer Willie Pastrano, until the fighter is able to punch rapid combinations with what D'Amato called "bad intentions." The theory behind the style is that when combined with effective bobbing and weaving head movement, the fighter has a very strong defense and becomes more elusive, able to throw hooks and uppercuts with great effectiveness. Also it allows swift neck movements as well quick duckings and bad returning damage, usually by rising uppercuts or even rising hooks.[[114]](#cite_note-Argo-116) Since it is a defense designed for close range fighting, it is mainly used by in-fighters. [Bobo Olson](/wiki/Bobo_Olson "Bobo Olson") was the first known champion to use this as a defense.

**[Crab Style Guards](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style) "Crab Defense (boxing style)")**: Work at all ranges, allowing fighters to defend while countering—such as using a lead arm to block jabs while keeping the rear hand free to punch. The style adapts to different boxing approaches: infighters use it to advance safely, out-boxers rely on one-handed defense to strike while evading, and sluggers use it to cover up after missed power shots. Its flexibility makes it effective for both offense and defense. The many variations of this defense include:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Boxing_at_the_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Girls%27_flyweight_Gold_Medal_Bout_113.jpg/250px-Boxing_at_the_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Girls%27_flyweight_Gold_Medal_Bout_113.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxing_at_the_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Girls%27_flyweight_Gold_Medal_Bout_113.jpg)

Boxer (right) using Cross-armed guard

* **[Cross-armed guard](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Cross-armed_guard "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** (sometimes known as the armadillo) - the forearms are placed on top of each other horizontally in front of the face with the glove of one arm being on the top of the elbow of the other arm.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) This style is greatly varied when the back hand (right for an orthodox fighter and left for a southpaw) rises vertically. In some cases, one hand is across the face with the forearm horizontal or diagonal. While the other lies low, protecting the body.[[115]](#cite_note-archie-117) This style is used for reducing head damage at close range, but can be used to defend the body as well.[[115]](#cite_note-archie-117) The only head punch that a fighter is susceptible to is a punch to the top of the head.[[115]](#cite_note-archie-117) The body is open if the guard is kept high, but most fighters who use this style bend and lean to protect the body, but while upright and unaltered the body is there to be hit. This position can be difficult to counterpunch from for beginners, but can be highly effective for counterpunching by more experienced fighters.[[116]](#cite_note-cross_arm-118) It also virtually eliminates all head damage. In close range a slightly crouched posture can be used and usually a front foot heavy squared stance.[[117]](#cite_note-genius-119) Meaning that the now protected head of the boxer, is a closer target than the body. However, this guard is also effective in a bladed stance and while moving or leaning backwards to block an opponent's counterpunches after a missed punch.[[118]](#cite_note-how_to-120)[[117]](#cite_note-genius-119)
* **[Reverse cross-armed guard](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Reverse_cross-armed_guard "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** - The forearms can be placed on top of each other horizontally or diagonally in front of the face with the lead arm (left for an orthodox fighter and right for a southpaw) being on the top of the rear arm with lead glove over the rear shoulder.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) The position of the lead arm (left for an orthodox fighter and right for a southpaw) is greatly varied when it rises vertically.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Floyd_Mayweather%2C_Jr._vs._Juan_Manuel_M%C3%A1rquez.jpg/250px-Floyd_Mayweather%2C_Jr._vs._Juan_Manuel_M%C3%A1rquez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Floyd_Mayweather,_Jr._vs._Juan_Manuel_M%C3%A1rquez.jpg)

[Floyd Mayweather Jr.](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather_Jr. "Floyd Mayweather Jr.") (left) using Michigan Defense against [Juan Manuel Márquez](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_M%C3%A1rquez "Juan Manuel Márquez")

* **[Philly Shell or Michigan Defense](/wiki/Crab_Defense_(boxing_style)#Philly_shell_or_Michigan_Defense "Crab Defense (boxing style)")** — This is a variation of the cross-armed guard.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) The lead arm (left for an orthodox fighter and right for a southpaw) is placed across the abdomen, below the rear arm, to protect the body.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) The head is titled towards the rear shoulder to keep the head off of center-line, and to make space to use the shoulder to block.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) The lead shoulder is brought in tight against the side of the face.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) The rear hand can be placed next to the chin close to the rear shoulder (right side for orthodox fighters and left side for southpaws) to defend against hook punches, placed in a cross block position, with the rear hand over the lead shoulder to protect against straight punches, or on the centerline to be able to rotate between a hook cover and a cross block or punch catch position.[[102]](#cite_note-aggresive-104) This style is used by fighters who like to counterpunch. To execute this guard a fighter must be very athletic and experienced. This style is so effective for counterpunching because it allows fighters to slip punches by rotating and dipping their upper body and causing blows to glance off the fighter. After the punch glances off, the fighter's back hand is in perfect position to hit their out-of-position opponent.[[120]](#cite_note-aggressive53-122) The weakness to this style is that when a fighter is stationary and not rotating they are open to be hit so a fighter must be athletic and well conditioned to effectively execute this style. To beat this style, fighters like to jab their opponents shoulder causing the shoulder and arm to be in pain and to demobilize that arm. But if mastered and perfected it can be an effective way to play defense in the sport of boxing.

**[Long guards](/wiki/Long_Guards_(boxing_style) "Long Guards (boxing style)")** also knows as Extended Guard: In boxing these guards are often used by taller fighters or fighters with longer reach to keep opponents out of punching range, but shorter fighters or fighters with shorter reach often use them intermittently.[[121]](#cite_note-mummy-123)[[122]](#cite_note-boxing2-124)[[123]](#cite_note-guard-125) Variations include:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._%2828485593653%29.jpg/250px-Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._%2828485593653%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_2016_-_Boxe-Boxing._(28485593653).jpg)

Boxers using Classic Long Guard

* **[Classic Long Guard](/wiki/Long_Guards_(boxing_style)#Classic_long_guard "Long Guards (boxing style)")** - The is a hybrid guard that combines the extended lead arm of the mummy guard with the rear hand in a classic guard, typically positioned at a 90-degree angle near the face.[[122]](#cite_note-boxing2-124)[[123]](#cite_note-guard-125) Advantages include the lead hand controls distance, blocks vision, parries, traps hands, and frames.[[122]](#cite_note-boxing2-124) The rear hand remains ready for power punches and defends against hooks.[[124]](#cite_note-126)[[125]](#cite_note-127) Disadvantages include a weak passive defense against uppercuts and straights that bypass the lead arm.[[126]](#cite_note-dracula-128) Powerful lead hooks and uppercuts are harder to throw since the arm must retract first, telegraphing the punch. It exposes the lead side of the body and allows opponents to gauge reach and distance easily.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Boxing_scene_Louvre_G384.jpg/250px-Boxing_scene_Louvre_G384.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxing_scene_Louvre_G384.jpg)

Boxer (left) using Dracula guard and Boxer (right) using Mummy guard on pottery dated to 470 BC

* **[Mummy Guard](/wiki/Long_Guards_(boxing_style)#Mummy_guard "Long Guards (boxing style)")** is a boxing stance where both arms are extended with slightly bent elbows and palms facing the opponent, while the chin is tucked and shoulders are raised for protection.[[121]](#cite_note-mummy-123) This guard allows fighters to block their opponent's vision and smother jabs, particularly against Classic or Peek-a-boo guards, though it is less effective against low-hand styles like the Crab Guards.[[121]](#cite_note-mummy-123) Taller fighters benefit from this stance as it discourages hooks and uppercuts, while shorter fighters can adjust by raising their shoulders and tucking elbows.[[121]](#cite_note-mummy-123) However, the Mummy Guard limits power punches since strikes require retracting the arms first, telegraphing movements and leaving the lead side vulnerable. Additionally, opponents can exploit lateral movement to close the distance and land punches before the extended arms can react.[[127]](#cite_note-post-129)
* **[Dracula Guard](/wiki/Long_Guards_(boxing_style)#Dracula_guard "Long Guards (boxing style)")** - A hybrid boxing guard that combines elements of the extended lead arm of the Mummy guard and the rear hand in a Cross Guard positioned for defense.[[126]](#cite_note-dracula-128)[[122]](#cite_note-boxing2-124) Named for its resemblance to Dracula hiding behind a cape, it uses the lead arm to block vision, control distance, parry, and trap hands, while the rear hand remains ready for power punches and defense.[[126]](#cite_note-dracula-128) Advantages include that it is good for obscuring vision and setting up traps. Allows quick jabs and rear hand power punches. Protects against straight punches, hooks, and uppercuts. Disadvantages include it limits powerful lead hooks and uppercuts as it requires pulling the arm back first, telegraphing the strike. Exposes the lead side of the body and makes reach more predictable.[[127]](#cite_note-post-129)

**[Classic Guards](/wiki/Classic_Guards_(boxing_style) "Classic Guards (boxing style)")** or Basic Guards: The modern Classic Guards are often the first Guards taught to boxers as the initial guard position is easy to learn,[[128]](#cite_note-elite-130)[[129]](#cite_note-uk-131) and they are effective against haymakers,[[130]](#cite_note-132) which is the type of punch many untrained fighters and beginners use often.[[94]](#cite_note-bjj-96)[[95]](#cite_note-mma-97) Guards fitting into this category include:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/4th_Boxing_Gala_E._Mavropoulos20.JPG/250px-4th_Boxing_Gala_E._Mavropoulos20.JPG)](/wiki/File:4th_Boxing_Gala_E._Mavropoulos20.JPG)

Boxer (right) using a Traditional Guard

* **[Traditional Guard](/wiki/Classic_Guards_(boxing_style)#Traditional_guard "Classic Guards (boxing style)")** - This guard involves bending both arms at 90 degrees or less, with the lead arm extended slightly away from the head and the rear fist held near the chin or jaw.[[131]](#cite_note-evolve-133) This guard offers passive defense[[132]](#cite_note-straight-134) against hooks by using the gloves, forearms, and elbows to block,[[133]](#cite_note-study-135)[[134]](#cite_note-catching-136) while the bent-arm position allows for powerful punches and better visibility than other classic guards.[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137) However, it leaves the centerline exposed, requiring quick reflexes and active defense, like parries, against straight punches and uppercuts, which can be difficult to master due to the need for specific blocking.[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137)[[131]](#cite_note-evolve-133)[[136]](#cite_note-parry-138)[[134]](#cite_note-catching-136) The guard also limits close-range effectiveness and lateral movement, as the high hand position makes punches more predictable, and reliance on blocking with the hands can delay counterpunching opportunities.[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Boxing-04-Gaidar.jpg/250px-Boxing-04-Gaidar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxing-04-Gaidar.jpg)

Boxer using a Conventional Guard

* **[Conventional Guard](/wiki/Classic_Guards_(boxing_style)#Conventional_Guard "Classic Guards (boxing style)")** - This guard involves holding both arms bent at 90 degrees or less, with the lead arm guarding the side of the head and the rear fist near the face or chin, offering passive defense against hooks by using gloves and elbows while enabling powerful punches due to the bent-arm position.[[134]](#cite_note-catching-136) It benefits fighters with slower reflexes by keeping hands closer for quicker blocks and parries but limits visibility and leaves the centerline exposed, requiring active defense against straight punches and uppercuts.[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137)[[136]](#cite_note-parry-138)[[134]](#cite_note-catching-136) It lacks redundant defense lines, relying heavily on hand blocks, which can delay counterpunches and make fighters vulnerable to hand traps, framing, and predictable punches.[[108]](#cite_note-hand-110) Mastering this guard demands high defensive specificity despite its initial ease of learning.[[109]](#cite_note-apollo-111)[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/2011_boxing_event_in_Sto%C5%BEice_Arena-Dejan_zavec_IV.jpg/250px-2011_boxing_event_in_Sto%C5%BEice_Arena-Dejan_zavec_IV.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_boxing_event_in_Sto%C5%BEice_Arena-Dejan_zavec_IV.jpg)

Boxer (left) using a High Guard

* **[High Guard](/wiki/Classic_Guards_(boxing_style)#High_Guard "Classic Guards (boxing style)")** - This guard involves bending both arms at 90 degrees or less, positioning the gloves in front of the face at eyebrow level, with hands resembling holding binoculars or making a heart shape,[[131]](#cite_note-evolve-133) with raised shoulders to protect the jaw and elbows pressed together to block uppercuts.[[137]](#cite_note-spartacus-139) Its advantages include ease of learning, passive defense against straight punches, uppercuts, partial defense against hooks, and better power generation due to bent arms, while also protecting the centerline.[[134]](#cite_note-catching-136)[[135]](#cite_note-flinch-137) However, it limits visibility, allows opponents to close distance more easily, leaves ears and jaw exposed to hooks,[[138]](#cite_note-broke-140) and exposes the lower body to attacks, relying heavily on forearm blocking, which can cause cumulative damage.[[139]](#cite_note-types-141)[[140]](#cite_note-crucial-142) Additionally, it offers only one line of defense, makes counterpunching slower,[[109]](#cite_note-apollo-111)[[141]](#cite_note-legends-143) and leaves fighters vulnerable to hand traps,[[108]](#cite_note-hand-110) framing, and split guards, though skilled boxers can bait opponents into counterattacks.[[142]](#cite_note-pull-144)[[143]](#cite_note-title-145)

### Unorthodox strategies

* **Rope-a-dope**: Used by Muhammad Ali in his 1974 "[the Rumble in the Jungle](/wiki/The_Rumble_in_the_Jungle "The Rumble in the Jungle")" bout against George Foreman, the rope-a-dope method involves lying back against the ropes, covering up defensively as much as possible and allowing the opponent to attempt numerous punches. The back-leaning posture, which does not cause the defending boxer to become as unbalanced as he would during normal backward movement, also maximizes the distance of the defender's head from his opponent, increasing the probability that punches will miss their intended target. Weathering the blows that do land, the defender lures the opponent into expending energy while conserving his/her own. If successful, the attacking opponent will eventually tire, creating defensive flaws which the boxer can exploit. In modern boxing, the [rope-a-dope](/wiki/Rope-a-dope "Rope-a-dope") is generally discouraged since most opponents are not fooled by it and few boxers possess the physical toughness to withstand a prolonged, unanswered assault. Recently,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] however, [eight-division world champion](/wiki/Octuple_champion "Octuple champion") Manny Pacquiao skillfully used the strategy to gauge the power of welterweight titlist Miguel Cotto in November 2009. Pacquiao followed up the rope-a-dope gambit with a withering knockdown. Tyson Fury also attempted this against Francesco Pianeto but did not pull it off as smoothly.
* **Bolo punch**: Occasionally seen in Olympic boxing, the [bolo punch](/wiki/Bolo_punch "Bolo punch") is an arm punch which owes its power to the shortening of a [circular arc](/wiki/Circular_arc "Circular arc") rather than to transference of body weight; it tends to have more of an effect due to the surprise of the odd angle it lands at rather than the actual power of the punch. This is more of a gimmick than a technical maneuver; this punch is not taught, being on the same plane in boxing technicality as is the [Ali shuffle](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali"). Nevertheless, a few professional boxers have used the bolo-punch to great effect, including former [welterweight](/wiki/Welterweight "Welterweight") champions Sugar Ray Leonard, and [Kid Gavilán](/wiki/Kid_Gavil%C3%A1n "Kid Gavilán") as well as current British fighter [Chris Eubank Jr](/wiki/Chris_Eubank_Jr. "Chris Eubank Jr."). Middleweight champion [Ceferino Garcia](/wiki/Ceferino_Garcia "Ceferino Garcia") is regarded as the inventor of the bolo punch.

* [![Bolo punch](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Contre_bolo1.jpg/120px-Contre_bolo1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Contre_bolo1.jpg "Bolo punch")

  *[Bolo punch](/wiki/Bolo_punch "Bolo punch")*
* [![Overhand (overcut)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Drop1.jpg/120px-Drop1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drop1.jpg "Overhand (overcut)")

  *[Overhand (overcut)](/wiki/Overhand_(boxing) "Overhand (boxing)")*

* **Overhand**: The [overhand](/wiki/Drop_(boxing) "Drop (boxing)") is a punch, thrown from the rear hand, not found in every boxer's arsenal. Unlike the cross, which has a trajectory parallel to the ground, the overhand has a looping circular arc as it is thrown over the shoulder with the palm facing away from the boxer. It is especially popular with smaller stature boxers trying to reach taller opponents. Boxers who have used this punch consistently and effectively include former heavyweight champions [Rocky Marciano](/wiki/Rocky_Marciano "Rocky Marciano") and [Tim Witherspoon](/wiki/Tim_Witherspoon "Tim Witherspoon"), as well as MMA champions [Chuck Liddell](/wiki/Chuck_Liddell "Chuck Liddell") and [Fedor Emelianenko](/wiki/Fedor_Emelianenko "Fedor Emelianenko"). The overhand has become a popular weapon in other tournaments that involve fist striking. [Deontay Wilder](/wiki/Deontay_Wilder "Deontay Wilder") heavily favours and is otherwise known for knocking many of his opponents out with one of his right overhands.
* **Check hook**: A [check hook](/wiki/Check_hook "Check hook") is employed to prevent aggressive boxers from lunging in. There are two parts to the check hook. The first part consists of a regular hook. The second, trickier part involves the footwork. As the opponent lunges in, the boxer should throw the hook and pivot on his left foot and swing his right foot 180 degrees around. If executed correctly, the aggressive boxer will lunge in and sail harmlessly past his opponent like a bull missing a matador. This is rarely seen in professional boxing as it requires a great disparity in skill level to execute. Technically speaking it has been said that there is no such thing as a check hook and that it is simply a hook applied to an opponent that has lurched forward and past his opponent who simply hooks him on the way past. Others have argued that the check hook exists but is an illegal punch due to it being a pivot punch which is illegal in the sport. Floyd Mayweather Jr. employed the use of a check hook against Ricky Hatton, which sent Hatton flying head first into the corner post and being knocked down.

## Ring corner

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/2017-12-02_Tina_Rupprecht_-_Anne_Sophie_Da_Costa_-_DSC2771.jpg/250px-2017-12-02_Tina_Rupprecht_-_Anne_Sophie_Da_Costa_-_DSC2771.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017-12-02_Tina_Rupprecht_-_Anne_Sophie_Da_Costa_-_DSC2771.jpg)

Boxer [Tina Rupprecht](/wiki/Tina_Rupprecht "Tina Rupprecht") receiving instructions from her trainer while being treated by her cutman in the ring corner between rounds

In boxing, each fighter is given a corner of the ring where they rest in between rounds for one minute and where their trainers stand. Typically, three individuals stand in the corner besides the boxer; these are the trainer, the assistant trainer and the cutman. The trainer and assistant typically give advice to the boxer on what they are doing wrong as well as encouraging them if they are losing. The cutman is a cutaneous [doctor](/wiki/Physician "Physician") responsible for keeping the boxer's face and eyes free of cuts, blood and excessive swelling. This is of particular importance because many fights are stopped because of cuts or swelling that threaten the boxer's eyes.

In addition, the corner is responsible for stopping the fight if they feel their fighter is in grave danger of permanent injury. The corner will occasionally throw in a white towel to signify a boxer's surrender (the idiomatic phrase "to throw in the towel", meaning to give up, derives from this practice).[[144]](#cite_note-146) This can be seen in the fight between [Diego Corrales](/wiki/Diego_Corrales "Diego Corrales") and Floyd Mayweather. In that fight, Corrales' corner surrendered despite Corrales' steadfast refusal.

## Health concerns

See also: [Dementia pugilistica](/wiki/Dementia_pugilistica "Dementia pugilistica") and [The distance (boxing) § Distance change criticisms](/wiki/The_distance_(boxing)#Distance_change_criticisms "The distance (boxing)")

Participating in boxing causes physical injuries. [Injuries to the head](/wiki/Injuries_to_the_head "Injuries to the head") are most commonly experienced by participants.[[145]](#cite_note-147) [Deaths of boxers during or after a bout](/wiki/List_of_deaths_due_to_injuries_sustained_in_boxing "List of deaths due to injuries sustained in boxing") from injuries received in the ring do occur. A 2011 study of bouts from 1890 to 2011 calculated an average [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") of 13 participants per year for the years studied.[[146]](#cite_note-148) An Australian study published in the *[Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport](/wiki/Journal_of_Science_and_Medicine_in_Sport "Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport")* in 2022 found that efforts made to improve safety in the sport in 2011 were unsuccessful at preventing deaths of participants in the sport.[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-l384-150)

Striking an individual unconscious or even inflicting a [concussion](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion") could result in permanent [brain damage](/wiki/Brain_damage "Brain damage").[[149]](#cite_note-151) There is no clear division between the force required to knock a person out and the force likely to kill a person.[[150]](#cite_note-152) Additionally, contact sports, especially combat sports, are directly related to an irreversible neurological disease known as [chronic traumatic encephalopathy](/wiki/Chronic_traumatic_encephalopathy "Chronic traumatic encephalopathy") (CTE). This disease begins during the athlete's life and continues even after sports activity has ceased.

In March 1981, [neurosurgeon](/wiki/Neurosurgeon "Neurosurgeon") Fred Sonstein sought to use [CT scans](/wiki/CT_scan "CT scan") to track the degeneration of boxers' cognitive functions after seeing the decline of [Bennie Briscoe](/wiki/Bennie_Briscoe "Bennie Briscoe").[[151]](#cite_note-153) From 1980 to 2007, more than 200 amateur boxers, professional boxers and [Toughman](/wiki/Toughman_Contest "Toughman Contest") fighters died due to ring or training injuries.[[152]](#cite_note-154) In 1983, editorials in the *Journal of the [American Medical Association](/wiki/American_Medical_Association "American Medical Association")* called for a ban on boxing.[[153]](#cite_note-155) The editor, George Lundberg, called boxing an "obscenity" that "should not be sanctioned by any civilized society".[[154]](#cite_note-156) Since then, the [British](/wiki/British_Medical_Association "British Medical Association"),[[155]](#cite_note-157) [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_Medical_Association "Canadian Medical Association"),[[156]](#cite_note-158) and [Australian Medical Associations](/wiki/Australian_Medical_Association "Australian Medical Association") have called for bans on boxing.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

Supporters of the ban argue that boxing is the only sport in which hurting the other athlete is the goal. Bill O'Neill, boxing spokesman for the British Medical Association (BMA), has supported the BMA's proposed ban on boxing: "It is the only sport where the intention is to inflict serious injury on your opponent, and we feel that we must have a total ban on boxing."[[158]](#cite_note-160) Opponents respond that such a position is misguided opinion, stating that amateur boxing is scored solely according to total connecting blows with no award for "injury". They observe that many skilled professional boxers have had rewarding careers without inflicting injury on opponents by accumulating scoring blows and avoiding punches winning rounds scored 10–9 by the [10-point must system](/wiki/10-point_must_system "10-point must system"), and they note that there are many other sports where concussions are much more prevalent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the data show that the concussion rate in boxing is the highest of all contact sports.[[159]](#cite_note-161)[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[161]](#cite_note-163) In addition, repetitive and subconcussive blows to the head, and not just concussions, cause CTE,[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165)[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and evidence indicates that brain damage and the effects of CTE are more severe in boxing.[[168]](#cite_note-170)[[169]](#cite_note-171)[[170]](#cite_note-172)[[171]](#cite_note-173)[[172]](#cite_note-174)[[173]](#cite_note-175)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In 2007, one study of amateur boxers showed that protective headgear did not prevent brain damage,[[174]](#cite_note-176) and another found that amateur boxers faced a high risk of brain damage.[[175]](#cite_note-amatneuro-177) The Gothenburg study[*[needs context](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Provide_context_for_the_reader "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*] analyzed temporary levels of neurofilament light in [cerebrospinal fluid](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid "Cerebrospinal fluid") which they conclude is evidence of damage, even though the levels soon subside. More comprehensive studies of neurological function on larger samples performed by Johns Hopkins University in 1994 and accident rates analyzed by [National Safety Council](/wiki/National_Safety_Council "National Safety Council") in 2017 show amateur boxing is a comparatively safe sport due to the regulations of amateur boxing and a greater control of the athletes,[[176]](#cite_note-178) although the studies did not focus on CTE or its long-term effects. In addition, effective training methods and a short career can reduce the effects of brain damage.[[177]](#cite_note-179)

In 1997, the American Association of Professional Ringside Physicians was established to develop medical protocols, based on research and education, to prevent injuries in boxing.[[178]](#cite_note-180)[[179]](#cite_note-181)

Professional boxing is forbidden in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"),[[180]](#cite_note-182) [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), and [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"). It was banned in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") until 2007, when the ban was lifted, but strict restrictions, including four three-minute rounds for fights, were imposed.[[181]](#cite_note-183) Boxing was banned in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") from 1965 until the [fall of communism in 1991](/wiki/Fall_of_communism_in_Albania "Fall of communism in Albania"). [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") legalized professional boxing in December 2014.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

The International Boxing Association (AIBA) restricted the use of head guards for senior male competitors at the World Championships and the Olympics after 2013.[[183]](#cite_note-185)[[184]](#cite_note-186) A literature review study analyses present knowledge about protecting headgear and injury prevention in boxing to determine if injury risks associated with not head guard usage increased. The literature review indicates that head guards provide effective protection against lacerations and skull fractures. Therefore, AIBA's decision to terminate the head guard must be considered cautiously, and injury rates among (male) boxers should be continuously evaluated.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

Like other active and dynamic sports, boxing may be argued to provide some general health benefits, such as fat burning, increased muscle tone, strong bones and ligaments, cardiovascular fitness, muscular endurance, improved core stability, coordination and body awareness, strength and power, stress relief, and self-esteem, though it's unlikely these offset the much greater risks.

## Boxing Halls of Fame

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/90/Gene_Tunney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gene_Tunney.jpg)

A stamp honoring heavyweight champion [Gene Tunney](/wiki/Gene_Tunney "Gene Tunney")

The sport of boxing has two internationally recognized boxing halls of fame; the [International Boxing Hall of Fame (IBHOF)](/wiki/International_Boxing_Hall_of_Fame "International Boxing Hall of Fame")[[186]](#cite_note-188) and the Boxing Hall of Fame Las Vegas. The latter opened in [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") in 2013 and was founded by [Steve Lott](/wiki/Steve_Lott "Steve Lott"), former assistant manager for Mike Tyson.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

The [International Boxing Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Boxing_Hall_of_Fame "International Boxing Hall of Fame") (IBHOF) opened in [Canastota, New York](/wiki/Canastota,_New_York "Canastota, New York") in 1989. The first inductees in 1990 included Jack Johnson, [Benny Leonard](/wiki/Benny_Leonard "Benny Leonard"), [Jack Dempsey](/wiki/Jack_Dempsey "Jack Dempsey"), [Henry Armstrong](/wiki/Henry_Armstrong "Henry Armstrong"), [Sugar Ray Robinson](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson "Sugar Ray Robinson"), [Archie Moore](/wiki/Archie_Moore "Archie Moore"), and [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali"). Other world-class figures[[188]](#cite_note-190) include [Salvador Sanchez](/wiki/Salvador_Sanchez "Salvador Sanchez"), [Jose Napoles](/wiki/Jose_Napoles "Jose Napoles"), [Roberto "Manos de Piedra" Durán](/wiki/Roberto_Duran "Roberto Duran"), [Ricardo Lopez](/wiki/Ricardo_Lopez_(boxer) "Ricardo Lopez (boxer)"), [Gabriel "Flash" Elorde](/wiki/Gabriel_%22Flash%22_Elorde "Gabriel \"Flash\" Elorde"), [Vicente Saldivar](/wiki/Vicente_Saldivar "Vicente Saldivar"), [Ismael Laguna](/wiki/Ismael_Laguna "Ismael Laguna"), [Eusebio Pedroza](/wiki/Eusebio_Pedroza "Eusebio Pedroza"), [Carlos Monzón](/wiki/Carlos_Monz%C3%B3n "Carlos Monzón"), [Azumah Nelson](/wiki/Azumah_Nelson "Azumah Nelson"), [Rocky Marciano](/wiki/Rocky_Marciano "Rocky Marciano"), [Manny Pacquiao](/wiki/Manny_Pacquiao "Manny Pacquiao"), [Pipino Cuevas](/wiki/Pipino_Cuevas "Pipino Cuevas"), [Wilfred Benitez](/wiki/Wilfred_Benitez "Wilfred Benitez"), [Wilfredo Gomez](/wiki/Wilfredo_Gomez "Wilfredo Gomez"), [Felix Trinidad](/wiki/Felix_Trinidad "Felix Trinidad") and [Ken Buchanan](/wiki/Ken_Buchanan "Ken Buchanan"). The Hall of Fame's induction ceremony is held every June as part of a four-day event. The fans who come to Canastota for the Induction Weekend are treated to a number of events, including scheduled autograph sessions, boxing exhibitions, a parade featuring past and present inductees, and the induction ceremony itself.

The Boxing Hall of Fame Las Vegas features the $75 million ESPN Classic Sports fight film and tape library and radio broadcast collection. The collection includes the fights of many great champions, including: Muhammad Ali, Mike Tyson, George Foreman, Roberto Durán, Marvin Hagler, Jack Dempsey, Joe Louis, Joe Frazier, Rocky Marciano and Sugar Ray Robinson. It is this exclusive fight film library that will separate the Boxing Hall of Fame Las Vegas from the other halls of fame which do not have rights to any video of their sports. The inaugural inductees included Muhammad Ali, Henry Armstrong, [Tony Canzoneri](/wiki/Tony_Canzoneri "Tony Canzoneri"), [Ezzard Charles](/wiki/Ezzard_Charles "Ezzard Charles"), Julio César Chávez Sr., Jack Dempsey, Roberto Durán, Joe Louis, and Sugar Ray Robinson.[[189]](#cite_note-191)
